name: Wasmer
summary: Multi-backend WebAssembly runtime: SinglePass (fast compile, low quality), Cranelift (mid-tier), and LLVM (peak quality, slow compile) backends are swappable per workload. The runtime's design philosophy is pick-compile-vs-quality per use case rather than committing to one tier.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Wasmer is a multi-backend WebAssembly runtime designed to let users pick their compile-time-versus-code-quality trade-off per workload. The available backends are:

- **SinglePass**: a fast streaming compiler that produces machine code with minimal optimisation. Compile time is near-instant; code quality is roughly at the level of Wasm-baseline JIT output.
- **Cranelift**: the same Cranelift used by Wasmtime — moderate compile time, good code quality, well-tested register allocator.
- **LLVM**: full LLVM optimisation pipeline. Highest peak performance, multi-second compile times per module.

The choice is per-use-case: for an edge platform serving short-lived requests, SinglePass minimises cold-start latency; for a long-running server processing many requests through the same Wasm module, LLVM's optimisation pays off.

sections.title: Production and trade-offs
sections.content:
Wasmer is used in commercial deployments at Wasmer Edge, several CDN serverless platforms, and various plug-in systems for desktop applications. The multi-backend design is its distinctive feature versus other Wasm runtimes (which typically commit to one backend choice).

The trade-off is **triple maintenance burden**: every change to the runtime must work across SinglePass, Cranelift, and LLVM backends, with their differing capability sets and bugs. The benefit is that users can evaluate compile-time-vs-quality trade-offs without changing runtimes.
